question: What specific language does the filing use regarding timing of Starship commercial payload delivery, V3 satellite deployment, and V2 Mobile deployment? Compare the wording and assess what it suggests about execution and timing risk.
facts: The filing states regarding Starship commercial payload delivery: 'We expect Starship to commence payload delivery to orbit in the second half of 2026.'
The Starship payload delivery timeline is conditioned on 'following additional flight tests'.
The filing acknowledges that 'delays or challenges in the Starship program have in the past occurred, and may occur in the future'.
The filing acknowledges that rocket programs 'have historically required substantial time and resources' to reach commercial viability.

The filing states regarding V3 satellite deployment: 'We expect to commence deploying our next-generation V3 satellites, designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite, using Starship in the second half of 2026.'
V3 satellite capacity of one Tbps per satellite is described as 'designed to offer', indicating a design target rather than demonstrated performance.
V3 deployment is explicitly conditioned on Starship readiness.
The filing states: 'Our current operational rockets, including Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy, are not capable of deploying V3 satellites'.

The filing states regarding V3 deployment de-risking: 'Full reusability of Starship's upper stage is not required to deploy our V3 satellites… In-orbit refueling is also not required for any of these LEO programs'.
The filing states regarding V2 Mobile deployment: 'We plan to expand our mobile constellation by deploying our next-generation mobile V2 Mobile satellites in 2027.'
The definitions section states: "'V2 Mobile satellites' refers to our next-generation mobile satellites… which we expect to begin deploying on Starship in 2027."
V2 Mobile deployment uses the verb 'plan to' rather than 'expect', signaling intent rather than expectation.
V2 Mobile deployment targets the full year '2027', providing less granularity than the 'second half of 2026' window used for Starship and V3.
The filing does not provide a commercial service launch date for V2 Mobile.

V2 Mobile deployment has five material prerequisites: Starship operational readiness, V2 Mobile satellites in orbit, EchoStar spectrum acquisition completion, U.S. regulatory authorizations, and international regulatory approvals.
The filing states regarding international regulatory approvals for V2 Mobile: 'cannot predict when or whether approvals will be granted in any given market'.

The filing states regarding 5G NR-NTN compliance for V2 Mobile: 'achieving full 5G NR-NTN compliance and optimal performance would likely require handset manufacturers to implement hardware and software modifications… There can be no assurance that these modifications will be adopted on our preferred timeline, or at all'.
The filing states: 'Our current operational rockets, including Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy, are not capable of deploying… V2 Mobile satellites'.
The progression from 'expect' (Starship, V3) to 'plan' (V2 Mobile) reflects a filing convention that suggests management has lower conviction on the V2 Mobile timeline.
The 6-month windows for Starship and V3 suggest more developed internal planning; the 12-month window for V2 Mobile suggests less schedule visibility.
Starship payload delivery has one precondition (additional flight tests), V3 has one (Starship readiness), and V2 Mobile has at least five independent prerequisites.
All three programs depend on Starship, and V2 Mobile depends additionally on regulatory and spectrum outcomes outside SpaceX's control, meaning V2 Mobile risk is compounded.
The filing does not provide a Starship commercial service ramp schedule, a V3 deployment completion date or volume target, or any date for V2 Mobile commercial service launch.

The de-risking language stating full upper-stage reusability and in-orbit refueling are not required for LEO programs suggests awareness that those milestones carry higher risk and an effort to decouple near-term satellite deployment from long-term Starship goals.

question: Assume a hypothetical year in which orbital AI compute satellite manufacturing costs total $2B. Quantify the difference in AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA between capitalization and expensing treatment and identify which disclosed accounting policies would govern the judgment.

The useful life for first-generation mobile satellites is 3 years.

The key judgment threshold is whether satellites are commercially operational and generating revenue versus in the development stage.
